Reverse Cavity
REVERSE CAVITY

What’s Next Proposal

The next phase: the work that turns the alignment into a brand the market can see.

Prepared by [Author - to be filled]
June 2026

0. The Commitment

Bottom line. The next phase builds the proof and the channels the brand’s positioning now rests on. We commission an independent before-and-after cohort that turns the founder’s single documented reversal into transferable proof, surface the founder’s purpose on the site, and stand up demand beyond the one decaying video. The proof cohort is the work that unlocks the rest. It starts on a decision, not a partner conversation.

The strategic choice this phase operationalizes is the one the Brief settled: re-anchor Reverse Cavity as the most-proven, fully-guided expression of remineralization, aimed at the faith-conscious natural-health buyer who has lost trust in conventional dentistry. That position holds only if the proof behind it is independent and repeatable, the purpose behind it is visible, and the demand behind it does not depend on a single viral asset. Each initiative below names work this firm performs, the decision it waits on, and where it sits in the sequence.

1. Initiative Cards

Initiative 1. The independent proof cohort

  1. The Move. We recruit, supervise, and document a cohort of 10 to 20 Reverse Cavity customers with consented before-and-after dental records, converting the founder’s single dentist-confirmed reversal into proof that transfers to any buyer.
  2. Why Now. The brand’s whole credibility rests on dentist-verified proof, and today that proof is one man’s X-rays confirmed by one independent dentist. The Brief’s central call, leading on documented proof and complete guidance, cannot ship its strongest claim until the proof point generalizes beyond the founder. Every credentialed rival wins on borrowed authority. None can answer a documented multi-customer outcomes record.
  3. Scope. A recruited cohort of 10 to 20 consented customers; a practitioner protocol for capturing baseline and follow-up intraoral X-rays and clinical notes; signed consent and records-release for every participant; a structured outcomes write-up with anonymized before-and-after imagery cleared for marketing use; and a one-page summary of what the cohort does and does not establish, written so the claims team can use it without overreaching.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 5/5. It is the single asset that turns the positioning from a leans into a claim the brand can stand behind in market and in front of a regulator.

Effort. 4/5.

Sequence note. Do first. The proof-and-guidance hero rewrite, the trust scaffolding, and the second proof asset all wait on what this cohort produces. This is the only initiative here that needs no partner conversation to begin. It needs a decision and consent from the first participants. Delaying it leaves every downstream claim resting on a sample of one.

Initiative 2. The customer-story and primary-interview program

  1. The Move. We run a set of AI-led custom interviews with consented Reverse Cavity customers and publish the resulting stories as named, sourced first-party proof.
  2. Why Now. The pipeline reached its conclusions without a single first-party customer interview. Every customer signal behind the current positioning is a review, a reseller testimonial, or a third-party article, so the brand has never heard its own buyers describe the fear that brought them and the result that kept them. The faith-conscious natural-health buyer is moved by lived stories from people like them, and the brand owns none on the record.
  3. Scope. A target list drawn from the proof cohort and from repeat purchasers; an AI-led interview guide covering the trigger to buy, the experience of the guided protocol, and the outcome; consented, attributable transcripts; and a set of edited customer stories the messaging team can place on the site and in campaigns. The program confirms what actually drives purchase and retention for this buyer and produces the authentic voices the brand currently lacks.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5. The proof cohort verifies the dentistry; this verifies the buyer, and the two together close the evidence gap the whole engagement flagged.

Effort. 2/5.

Sequence note. Runs alongside the proof cohort and draws its first participants from it. The cohort confirms the clinical result; these interviews capture the human one.

Initiative 3. The founder’s-why brand layer and A/B test

The single uncopyable asset the brand owns is generating no market value because it is invisible on every customer surface. We fix that here.

  1. The Move. We draft and publish a founder’s-why brand layer on owned surfaces, then test it.
  2. Why Now. The Brief named the founder’s purpose, the late-wife legacy, the freedom-from-dentistry mission, the ministry funding, as the one differentiator no competitor can manufacture, sitting unused while the site leads with a results headline and “Shop now.” A credential war against credentialed rivals is unwinnable. This ground is open and it is the brand’s alone.
  3. Scope. A purpose-led About page and a founder-story brand layer carried through the homepage and protocol pages; copy written to honor the founder’s privacy around personal loss and faith while making the mission legible to a secular buyer; and an A/B test instrumenting conversion and retention against the current results-led variant.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5. No competitor can manufacture the founder’s purpose; only its effect on conversion is untested, and the test settles that.

Effort. 2/5.

Sequence note. Can run in parallel with the proof work. The test result decides how far the purpose layer leads.

Initiative 4. Third-party trust scaffolding

  • Scope first, because the gap shows on the site today. An aggregated, on-site review widget pulling verified purchases; real, populated brand social channels replacing the placeholder footer links; and a documented path to the independent or lab testing the remineralization category expects.
  1. The Move. We install the third-party trust apparatus the category treats as table stakes and the site currently lacks.
  2. Why Now. The brand’s external trust signals are thin: placeholder social links and no aggregated proof of customer volume, while every serious rival in the category carries them. A buyer arriving from a results claim finds nothing corroborating it. The scaffolding turns the proof cohort and the customer stories into signals a first-time visitor can see without being told.
  3. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5. Category-standard trust signals are low-risk and the brand visibly trails on them.

Effort. 3/5.

Sequence note. The review widget and social channels can start immediately. The independent-testing path waits on the proof cohort, which is the credible source for it.

Initiative 5. The second deliberate proof asset

  1. The Move. We produce a second proof-content piece engineered to draw traffic on its own merits, testing whether the founder can make credible first-person proof content on purpose rather than by luck.
  2. Why Now. The brand’s growth story rests on one video that drew roughly 480,000 views, not the one-to-two million the team believed, and that single channel is decaying. The real structural question is whether the founder’s ability to produce credible proof content repeats. One viral hit proved demand exists. It did not prove the company can make demand.
  3. Scope. One produced proof-content piece, scripted and shot to stand on the documented cohort results rather than on a dramatic absolute; a distribution plan that does not assume virality; and measured traffic and conversion against a defined baseline so the result reads as signal, not anecdote.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5. The single-channel dependence is a documented fragility, and this is the cheapest test of whether the founder’s proof content repeats.

Effort. 3/5.

Sequence note. Depends on the proof cohort, which gives the asset something true and dramatic to be about.

Initiative 6. The multi-channel go-to-market plan

  1. The Move. We stand up demand channels beyond the single video, each with its own traffic target and a defined fallback if it underperforms.
  2. Why Now. Self-reported revenue has already swung with the founder’s availability, the signature of a business that lives on one channel. The brand has treated deliberate go-to-market as unnecessary because the one asset carried it. That asset is decaying, so durable revenue now requires a channel mix the company builds on purpose. This is the strategy work the brand brought Maurice in to lead.
  3. Scope. A channel plan naming the specific channels to test for the faith-conscious natural-health buyer; a per-channel traffic and acquisition target; a budget allocation across them; and a kill-or-keep rule for any channel that does not return against its target.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5. Diversifying off a single decaying channel is the clear move; the open question is which channels win, which the plan tests rather than assumes.

Effort. 4/5.

Sequence note. Depends on the second proof asset performing without a viral fluke, which tells us there is repeatable content to put behind paid and organic channels.

Initiative 7. Beachhead sizing and acquisition test

  1. The Move. We size and test the faith-conscious natural-health audience on the Life & Health Network channel the founder’s proof already lives on, measuring acquisition cost against broad targeting.
  2. Why Now. The “everyone gets cavities, global seven-to-eight-figure” ambition is sound but mis-sized: the buyable remineralizing niche is low-single-billions, the clinical-burden number concentrates in low-income populations least able to buy a premium kit, and an incumbent already owns the full-catalog, multi-channel stack the brand only aspires to. Winning one reachable community first, on the proof and purpose incumbents lack, beats spreading scarce capital across a market a competitor already holds.
  3. Scope. A sizing estimate of the reachable faith-conscious audience on the Life & Health Network and adjacent channels; a controlled acquisition test measuring cost-per-customer there against broad targeting; and a decision memo resolving the beachhead-versus-broad question with the numbers in hand. The specific Seventh-day Adventist demographic stays an inference for leadership to confirm, not an asserted target.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 3/5. Beachhead-first is the disciplined sequence, but the niche size and the specific community remain client-data questions the test exists to answer.

Effort. 4/5.

Sequence note. Depends on the multi-channel plan, which frames the channels this test compares. The sequenced catalog expansion, the toothpaste, putty, and toothbrush line, waits behind this test and starts only once the beachhead proves its unit economics.

2. Sequencing

The order follows dependency and impact, entered by condition rather than calendar.

The proof cohort begins on a decision, because everything credible downstream draws on it. The customer-story program and the founder’s-why layer run alongside it, since one recruits from the cohort and the other touches a different surface entirely. The review widget and real social channels start in parallel, because they wait on nothing; the independent-testing leg of the trust scaffolding holds until the cohort gives it a credible source.

Once the cohort produces documented outcomes, the second proof asset has something true to be built on, and it ships next. When that asset draws traffic without a viral fluke, the multi-channel plan stands up behind it. The beachhead sizing and acquisition test runs once there are channels to compare, and the catalog expansion holds until the beachhead proves its unit economics. No later initiative starts before the evidence the earlier one produces is in hand.

3. What We Need From You

Each initiative starts on a specific input from leadership.

  • Proof cohort. A decision to commission it, the founder’s introduction to the first willing customers, and budget for the supervising practitioner and records handling.
  • Customer-story program. Approval to contact named customers and the consent standard the founder is comfortable with for attributable stories.
  • Founder’s-why layer. The founder’s decision on how much of the late-wife legacy, the faith, and the ministry purpose he will put on the public record, and sign-off to run the A/B test.
  • Trust scaffolding. The brand’s real social-channel handles and a decision on which independent or lab testing path to pursue once the cohort exists.
  • Second proof asset. The founder’s availability to be filmed and agreement on the documented result the piece is built around.
  • Multi-channel plan. A committed go-to-market budget and the kill-or-keep threshold leadership will hold each channel to.
  • Beachhead test. Confirmation of whether the Seventh-day Adventist community is the intended target or a starting hypothesis, and the founder’s read on the niche-size figure the test will check.
Reverse Cavity
